## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right. So, welcome to Total Breakthrough Training. Today we're going to be doing the one week four, and we're going to be doing a coach, a study buddy session. This is going to be a study buddy training session. I want to invite anyone who's on the phone to get to click the Webcast link. We're in front of a computer where you can see a slideshow that we put up that might help you with the structure of what we're doing today. And we're going to be training you in some of your study buddy exercises that you'll be doing with your study buddy, and specifically in one of the key skills of coaching, which is understanding, interviewing someone about their life and about their life story. Magalene, anything you'd like to add to that? Yeah, I think one of the reasons that we're introducing this at this point in our work together in Total Breakthrough is that after watching the roundtable sessions that Tony did, one of the takeaways Mark and I had was that Tony was gathering information in a really nice way about both Joaquin and Kim's life stories. And we thought about how getting this information from a client as a strategic intervention coach is really different than when other styles of coaching or therapy ask for a person's life story. So we're going to be presenting today a new unique way, really, to interview a person about their life story. And this is the work you're going to be doing with each other in your study buddy session. But it will incorporate a whole bunch of strategic intervention strategies. And one of the more important ways that we approach people and their lives in strategic intervention is that the coach has some independence in figuring out what is important to the client. So there are some schools of coaching where, for instance, you're not supposed to just guide with questions. And there's other schools of coaching where you're supposed to let the client come to their own conclusions. There's also therapy that when someone tells you a story, you put a certain cast on, a certain interpretation or certain templates for understanding what someone's past is, and you give it a diagnosis. And so in strategic intervention, there's other types of coaching where people stay really on the surface and they really focus on, say, a goal that you want and they never dig into the person's life, their circumstances, their relationships, their history, and really all the kind of juicy energy of life that that person has, they're leaving that untouched. And because they're leaving that untouched, they're also not discovering conflicts that that person might have. They're not discovering that deep well of desire that people have that helps them grow.

You'll also start to understand were there some key decisions that your client made that really shaped the way they can grow right now and the direction you can take in their coaching? Did they make a key decision to not try very hard in a certain part of life? Or could they have perhaps skipped the life stage? Some people skip the life stage of leaving home and they stay home. For someone else, they may have had the type of childhood that they took care of siblings, and they didn't feel that they had enough time playing. So how does that relate to the client that you're working with, their mode of being? ## [06:02 - 09:02]

And so the structure of today will be that we'll go through these six strategies rather quickly, and then we'll ask for a volunteer to go through some exercises with us where you'll see some of these strategies being used. So the six strategies are the elevation strategy, life stages, key decisions, finding the spark, bright spot, and mode of being. And so why don't we start off with each of these in turn and just do a quick summary of these, Matt? Absolutely. And also I just wanted to mention that even though these are all individual strategies and one could have a session, really focused on one of these strategies, while you interview a person about their life, these strategies can naturally become a part of the session. And you also may not use all of them. This isn't a right or wrong approach. These are just the tools you have as a strategic interventionist. Also if I could add, these are used, yeah, these don't require a session, so you can use them in any circumstance, basically, when you're talking to people. Yes, especially this elevation strategy. I'm going to let Mark describe it, and then I'm going to give my two cents about it. Okay. Great. So the elevation strategy, you know, anytime when a client is speaking to you, they're revealing things to you about themselves, right? They're revealing their needs, their wants, their desires, their relationships, their circumstances. And anything that they reveal to you is subject to an emotional interpretation, right? So when someone tells you about something that happened as a child, there is an emotional element where it's like either it's... People really have this apprehension, like it's either good or it's bad or it's suspect or it's weird. And so this emotional interpretation is really in the coach's control. So when you're receiving information from your client, the way that you receive that, you can use to elevate the person. So the elevation strategy is how do you find the good intention and strength in what your client reveals? So this builds trust, and it will help you discover the client's strength that will enable them to overcome any challenges now. And this is just, for instance, we all have seen movies about bad therapists who, as soon as someone tells something about them, they come back and say, well, you have an edifice syndrome, or they start diagnosing the person or labeling them. That's the opposite of elevation strategy, because you're basically telling someone that whatever experience they're having, it's already in a box, it's already been labeled by someone else, and it means all sorts of things about them. The elevation strategy is the opposite. When you take...you want to find a way to build a stronger, positive intent with a person. So when they bring something up, they may have question marks about the past. They may have question marks about their feelings or their desires, and you want to strengthen those so that question mark is entered by an affirmative

## [09:02 - 12:03]

that builds on their positive intent, their stronger identity, a stronger history, and stronger possibilities for themselves. So one way that I like to think about this is that your...like Mark said, you're building up the person's identity as you speak to them with all kinds of little positive things that you built into the conversation. And if someone's really low about themselves, then I would take it a step further and actually have them list for me all of the strengths that they see in them. So that would be in a sense, a deeper way to use the elevation strategy, where if I'm not hearing any positive buildup in them of feeling their own personal power, their own way they relate to others that is strong, I might ask them to just list for me all the ways that they do good in the world being just who they are. But another type of example would be, you see it on your screen now, if the client says, I stayed home and took care of my little sister all of the time when she's talking about her childhood, then the coach might say, you must have been really mature to be able to do this. And the client might come back at you with, no, my mom was just irresponsible. They don't want to take credit. They might actually have guilt about the way they were as an older sibling. But then you want to reinforce the good and say, I'm sure there was a strong part of you that was there for your sister. So many times a coach has an opportunity to see this better side of the person they're working with and help them find the strength in them to then be able to do the work they have to do now to reach their goals. Or to improve their relationships. Exactly. And so I love that example of that interaction because someone says, I stayed home and took care of my sister and their predominant meeting right now might be blame at their mother, for instance. But the coach, using the elevation strategy, turns that into a solid strength. And then later on, when the client is really faced with something new that they need to do, the coach can remind them of that strength and say, you know what, just like that part of you that was there for your sister, just like this and you're stacking and building on a success. So it's really effective to get that person to a higher baseline in terms of what they can accomplish. And this is something you can practice with friends, with children, with spouses. Just being in a conversation that's not a coaching conversation where you're in the mode of elevating the person. So feel free to just say to a study buddy, for example, let's just practice elevating each other for five minutes. Yeah.

## [12:03 - 15:07]

Great. All right. So next stage and next strategy is life stages. Now, this is another way that strategic intervention is very different from other types of coaching. We believe that when people have a request within a coaching for something in their life, it takes place within the context of the life stage. So throughout life, we're confronted with new situations and needs and challenges based on our age or health or relationship cycle or professional development. There are all sorts of cues. But basically what happens is that, and this is when people say that life is always changing, really our life stages are always changing. So one day you can wake up in a new life stage where there's something you want and need some today that you didn't feel like you needed yesterday. So, you know, I mean, we hear about the prototypical people, they wake up and suddenly they want more success in their career, or suddenly they want a family and a baby. Suddenly they want a relationship. Suddenly they want to have more money at their bank account. And maybe the day before, they didn't really care about that. That wasn't on the horizon for them. And so we all have these – so basically when someone is – again, there's types of coaching and therapy where if someone is depressed, you say, oh, well, let's see if you're depressed. And they don't differentiate between a 15-year-old's depression and a 55-year-old's depression. And they're, of course, completely different. Why are they different? Because in order to help that person feel great in their life stage, they have to do completely different things. It's a completely different situation, right? So by understanding the life stage, you can discover and find one to three priorities that would significantly change the client's life. And you find those things and you'll know how to help that person. So some examples of – yeah, go ahead. You know, an example that I often think about that might be a little different than what's on the screen right now is that a typical life stage of having a baby when suddenly a woman feels an incredible need for certainty and to be taken care of maybe by her husband or for herself to be able to provide for her child. And this could have been someone that before then had very different needs, a very adventurous person. And so immediately there's a new challenge in the relationship with the husband because there's been a shift in life stage. And the mother is reacting in a way that the husband could not have easily predicted. Yeah. And what's really important about that also is that people switch. So you can have people who have a certain kind of chemistry in one life stage and in another life stage they have a very different chemistry, right? So when people – especially in the first 18 months of having a child or maybe the – plus the nine months of pregnancy, something like that, two years it's really a roller coaster ride where everything can change for the couple.

## [15:07 - 18:11]

And so really understanding the life stages and how to serve each other and how to navigate that is really a critical skill. And it's really important for coaches to understand as well. So, you know, for instance, let's do some examples of life stages and priorities. There's a life stage of leaving home, right? And I call this one out because it's one that's familiar to all of us and, you know, it's typical in our – And by the way, Jay Haley wrote an entire book called Leaving Home, right? That's right. That's right. And so three main priorities are, one, get basic life skills for living away from home, right? So basically being able to live away from home, being able to think about money and manage things, right? That's a basic life skill. Two is find friends, peers, social life, and a relationship, right? So when you move away from home you have people that you're connecting with and relationships that you're fostering. And three is to set up some course of learning or life directions like a school or a job or a compelling goal. So you – the young person has something that they're looking forward to or they're working towards. So these are very, very practical, you know, the priorities here. And the reason that they're practical is that if you can succeed at these, you're pretty much ready for the next life stage. If someone is leaving home and they're away from home and they don't have life skills and they can't somehow get a job or handle money, or they are completely isolated and have no friends and no one knows them, or if they have, you know, they have a job and they have friends but they have no sense of where they're going in life, well then you can see how that can become a challenge, maybe not immediately, but at some point when they look at the next stage of life they may be saying, wow, you know, I never really tackled that. I really need to handle it now. One thing also, Magalie, the way that you look at life stages that we've been talking about is that, you know, very often people feel like they have life stages that they want to recover from the past or that they have different feelings about life stages that they want to get back, right? Yeah, and I think this comes out a lot when you talk about a person's life and their past. You might hit upon a certain stage that they went through too quickly. I have a friend that was telling me about her life story and she said that at age 21 she met an older man, he was maybe in his mid-30s, they were married very quickly, he had older children, and she went from being in her final year of college to being a mother of, I think, like an eight-year-old or something like this, and so she skipped an entire life stage of being a young woman getting a job, falling in love, and having a family on her own for the first time.

## [18:11 - 21:19]

She jumped into his life that was more mature. So that's a typical thing that will happen and your client may or may not see that as a negative, and you don't have to see it ever as a negative, but sometimes people need an explanation of why now they may be yearning for something that seems immature to them, or they want to have an experience that doesn't fit in with how other people around them see where they should be at. For example, sometimes people want to take a trip around the world and their friends say, but you're too old to do that. That would be a negative. Or guys in their 50s like to drive around in Ferraris and people say, you're too old. That's a whole other lecture. That's a whole other life stage. But it's a life stage issue, so you will sometimes find that people have desires that are bound with a life stage or that are connected to a life stage. So another example, obviously, is Joaquin, a late career athlete. He's 36. One of the lessons that Tony had, or the points that Tony made with him about his career, is that there are certain things that pro athletes need to do. They have their very foreshortened careers, right? I mean, they have maybe a 10-15 year career of a pro athlete. So one, they need to set aside earnings from their peak earnings years. So we all hear about athletes who spend every dime as it comes through, and then their career is over in seven to 10 or 15 years, whatever, or cut short by an injury, and then they have problems. The second one is reset their priorities and needs so that they can draw satisfaction and fulfillment from post-athletic life. This is anyone who's been a celebrity or been in an intense public situation where they get a lot of attention, is that they may, you know, they basically will move to another phase where they don't have the same levels of significance. They may not be the top of the pile, and they need to have strong priorities and needs so that they're connected to something else in their life in another way of contributing and another way of growing. And the third is a business consideration for an athlete is to capitalize on a prestige and reputation to build the new career opportunities and profit centers. So in Joaquin's case, for instance, he was working on that with his camps, right? I mean, people knew who Joaquin Hawkins was. He was a locally well-known celebrity, so he built a camp out of that. So this is just an example. Three priorities that any athlete who's in their late 30s has got to consider. And as a coach, if you understand that you're dealing with an athlete, we also do have a lot of people in our program who are athletic coaches who have that crisis happen for the athlete in their 30s, where suddenly the things that motivated them in their early 20s that drive and they're given up on having a personal life or having a family,

## [21:19 - 24:21]

and in the 30s they really want that and suddenly they're blaming their career. So these are life stages to consider. There's a life stage of having school-aged children. There's a life stage of launching your children out into the world and being the parent of an adult.
